narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—above the typical 3.21 average—and middle-family income background, you've shown real determination in pursuing law school. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam outcomes based on students' academic and background profiles, estimated a 70% chance you'll fail the bar on your first try. This high predicted risk led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass and succeed in practice. The model's decision hinged most on your LSAT score of 32, well below the average of 36.70, which stood out as the strongest factor pushing toward a failure prediction by signaling potential challenges in the rigorous legal reasoning needed for the bar. Next in influence was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 8th—higher than the average 5.53—which worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward passing by highlighting your later academic improvement. Your middle-income family bracket also played a role, contributing somewhat toward the failure risk, possibly reflecting patterns in the data where such backgrounds correlate with added pressures. Compared to typical students, your full-time enrollment plans align with the vast majority (over 92%), so that didn't sway things much, but your LSAT pulled hardest against you despite strengths like your undergrad performance and Year 3 gains. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man committed to law—many in similar positions overcome these hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, as that's the biggest lever here. Strengthening recent academic deciles further could help too. Reach out to admissions for feedback, explore prep courses tailored for underrepresented students, or apply to programs with holistic reviews that weigh your unique story more heavily. With targeted steps, you can reshape this narrative and get back on track.
